coredata.dc:title: In-situ stabilizing surface oxygen vacancies of TiO2 nanowire array photoelectrode by N-doped carbon dots for enhanced photoelectrocatalytic activities under visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shanpeng
Liu, Chunlei
Chen, Ping
Lv, Wenying
Liu, Guoguang
coredata.dc:description:
Oxygen vacancy was considered to act as electron scavengers, delaying electron-hole pair recombination for their critical impacts on the electronic-band structural modulation and optical absorption of a semiconductor. However, the generation and stabilization of oxygen vacancies (Vo) remains a considerable challenge. Herein, a N-doped carbon dots/Vo-rich TiO2 nanowire array (NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo) photoelectrode with a stable oxygen vacancy layer in TiO2 energy band was constructed successfully by doping with NCDs. This optimized NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo photoelectrode exhibited high activity and durability with a degradation reaction constant that was ∼ two and three fold higher than that of TiO2 NA-Vo and TiO2 NA electrodes, respectively, and maintained a high degradation efficiency after eight consecutive degradation cycles. Moreover, the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo photoelectrode demonstrated a photocurrent density of 0.56 mA cm−2, which was more than three times that of TiO2 NA electrode. These excellent visible light-driven photoelectrocatalysis (PEC) activities could be attributed to the stabilized and regulated oxygen vacancy layer induced by NCDs, which further accelerated the interfacial charge separation to generate more free radicals (e.g.,
OH, O2
−, and 1O2) confirmed by electron-spin resonance (ESR) and Photoluminescence Spectra (PL). Our work provides an effective strategy to regulate the defected TiO2 electrodes in the applications of visible light-driven wastewater treatment.

head.ce:title: In-situ stabilizing surface oxygen vacancies of TiO2 nanowire array photoelectrode by N-doped carbon dots for enhanced photoelectrocatalytic activities under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Guangdong University of Technology, Guangzhou 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Drinking Water Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Drinking Water Science and Technology, Research Center for Eco-Environmental Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100085, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Oxygen vacancy was considered to act as electron scavengers, delaying electron-hole pair recombination for their critical impacts on the electronic-band structural modulation and optical absorption of a semiconductor. However, the generation and stabilization of oxygen vacancies (Vo) remains a considerable challenge. Herein, a N-doped carbon dots/Vo-rich TiO2 nanowire array (NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo) photoelectrode with a stable oxygen vacancy layer in TiO2 energy band was constructed successfully by doping with NCDs. This optimized NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo photoelectrode exhibited high activity and durability with a degradation reaction constant that was ∼ two and three fold higher than that of TiO2 NA-Vo and TiO2 NA electrodes, respectively, and maintained a high degradation efficiency after eight consecutive degradation cycles. Moreover, the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo photoelectrode demonstrated a photocurrent density of 0.56 mA cm−2, which was more than three times that of TiO2 NA electrode. These excellent visible light-driven photoelectrocatalysis (PEC) activities could be attributed to the stabilized and regulated oxygen vacancy layer induced by NCDs, which further accelerated the interfacial charge separation to generate more free radicals (e.g.,
OH, O2
−, and 1O2) confirmed by electron-spin resonance (ESR) and Photoluminescence Spectra (PL). Our work provides an effective strategy to regulate the defected TiO2 electrodes in the applications of visible light-driven wastewater treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, energy crisis and environmental contamination have urged the rapid development of ecofriendly and renewable energy sources [1,2]. As a promising renewable and clean energy sources, solar energy can be captured by semiconductor photocatalysts to achieve the efficient conversion of solar energy. As an ideal photocatalyst and photoelectrode, titanium dioxide (TiO2) has been widely employed in these field, encompassing photocatalytic hydrogen generation, organic contaminant remediation, as well as dye-sensitized solar cells, due to its non-toxicity, physical, and photochemical stability [3–7]. However, pristine TiO2, with a wide band gap (3.2 eV), can only utilize <385 nm UV light of the sunlight spectrum. As is well known, sunlight is comprised of 5% UV light, 40% visible light, and 55% near infrared (NIR) light; thus, effective strategies that might extend the usable solar spectrum to the visible, and even NIR regions, warrant intensive study [8]. Furthermore, the rapid recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in TiO2 energy band severely restricts its practical application [9]. Consequently, numerous methods have been developed to narrow the band gap, or prolong the separation of photo-generated charge carriers, such as doping [10,11], forming defects, or hydrogenation [12,13].

Oxygen vacancies (Vo), one of the most important defects in metal oxides, have been extensively studied in photocatalysts [14,15]. Various theoretical calculations and experimental characterizations have revealed that the introduction of oxygen vacancies as well as the generation of Ti3+ centers in TiO2 energy band can create a mid-gap state under the conduction band, which effectively promoted the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs to improve the photocatalytic performance [16,17]. Hydrogenation, as an efficacious approach, has been widely applied to produce the oxygen vacancies or defects state of metal oxides [18,19]. However, Oxygen vacancies are usually present in bulk of catalysts rather than surface and ordered state, so that the vast majority of them may not be effectively involved in catalytic reactions [20,21]. Thus, modulating the structure of oxygen vacancy layer for keeping amount of surface oxygen vacancies at the optimized level would promote the activities of catalyst. An et al. reported a facile urea-assisted strategy to achieve the rearrangement of oxygen vacancies, which further alleviated the disadvantageous effects of bulk defects on charge separation. Benefiting from the diminishment of oxygen vacancy defects in the bulk, the as-synthesized junction exhibited superior activity for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution [22]. Li et al. reported a modified chemical vapor deposition (CVD) process, surface oxygen vacancies with different concentrations was regulated on layered MoO2 under reduction atmosphere. The suitable introduction of oxygen vacancies not only boost chemical-adsorption toward N2 via electron donation on Mo3+ ions, but lowered activation energy barrier, which make an NRR with high Faradic efficiency can be achieved under a quite low potential [23]. Inspired by the above impressive advances, the modulation and stabilization of oxygen vacancies will become an effective strategy to develop TiO2-based catalysts.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new member of carbon family, have attracted considerable attention due to their unique up-, down- converted photoluminescence (PL) properties [24,25]. Chen et al. synthesized the CQDs/TiO2 composite showed higher visible-light driven photocatalytic activity for the degradation of gemfibrozil (GEM) than that of pure TiO2. The reason was attributed that the TiO2 doped by CQDs not only improve the solar light absorption and conversion capacity (NIR light up-converted to visible light), but reduce the band gap of pristine TiO2 [26]. Due to fact that the nitrogen doping can efficiently induce charge delocalization and tune the work function of carbon, the electron transfer capability of carbon materials can be efficiently promoted [27–29]. Besides, the nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) can further extend absorption in the visible light region [30–33]. Thus, NCDs can be constructed and introduced to the semiconductor system for enhanced photocatalytic performance. Zhang et al. reported a kind of NCDs/TiO2 composite photocatalysts for enhanced photocatalytic activity and better dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) performance than that of N-free CQDs, which was attributed to their lower work function induced by N doping [34]. Sun et al. reported an ordered and porous NCDs-sensitized Bi2O3 inverse opal with enhanced photoelectrochemical performance and photocatalytic activity than that of CQDs/ Bi2O3, which owed that the NCDs can further extend absorption in the visible light region [35]. Although the NCDs or CQDs may be assembled on the surface of some photocatalysts to yield Vis-NIR light activated photocatalysts, the rapid recombination of photo-generated electron-hole pairs in the narrowed band gap restrict their photoactivity [36]. Thus, it is reasonable to hypothesis that engineering of based a stable middle layer for electron acceptor to delay electron-hole pair recombination in narrowed down energy band structure of NCDs modified TiO2 for promoting photocatalytic performance should be of great significance.

Long-term exposure to environmental tetracycline (TC) can result in antibiotic resistance, which poses serious threats to aquatic organisms and human health [37,38]. This contaminant is extremely recalcitrant to removal via conventional wastewater treatment technologies due to its stable chemical structure and high resistance to biodegradation [38–40]. Typically, photocatalytic (PC) techniques are employed for the degradation of pharmaceutical and personal care products (PPCPs); however, they usually were operated in suspensions, which seriously restricts the recyclability of the photocatalysts [41–43]. Electrochemical (EC) techniques are considered as alternative advanced oxidation processes (AOPs), due to their ease of operation and efficacy; however, methods for decreasing their energy consumption are lacking [44]. To further reduce electricity and improve degradation efficiency, updated photoelectrochemical (PEC) techniques with a highly efficient photoelectrode under sunlight irradiation of are under investigation.

Herein, we proposed the NCDs-assisted construction of a stable oxygen vacancy layer in TiO2 energy band for enhanced PEC activities under visible light. A facile hydrogen treatment method was used to regulate different concentrations of surface oxygen vacancies in TiO2 NA. In order to form a stable oxygen vacancy layer in TiO2 energy band, a novel NCDs-assisted strategy was developed to achieve in-situ stabilizing the surface oxygen vacancies of TiO2 NA. Based on the combined results of experimental characterizations and PEC degradation activities, the incorporation of NCDs reduced the band gap of TiO2 by doping, and improved the visible-light photocatalytic activity. Furthermore, they greatly restrained the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes through the assisted construction of a stabilized oxygen vacancy layer in TiO2 energy band. As a result, a highly efficient and recyclable NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo photoelectrode was obtained for the degradation of TC under visible light.

Ti foams were purchased from the Shanxi Baoji Metal Fabrication Company. Other regents of analytical grade were obtained from Afla Aesar (ACS reagent) and used without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ·cm−1) from a Milli-Q Plus system (Millipore) was used for experimental preparation, washing and dilution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of TiO2 NA-Vo and TiO2 NA electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: Vertically aligned TiO2 nanowire array (TiO2 NA) were fabricated on a Ti foam following the procedures. Firstly, commercial Ti foam (purity > 99.5%) with pore diameters of 30 μm was cut into pieces of 2 cm (width) × 4 cm (length) × 0.8 mm (thickness), and used as substrates or electrodes. Subsequently, the pretreated Ti foam was placed against the inner wall of a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, which was filled with 35 mL of a 4 M NaOH aqueous solution and preheated to 180 °C for 1 h. The synthesized Na2Ti3O7 arrays on the Ti foam were rinsed several times with ultrapure water and then immersed in a 0.25 M HNO3 solution for 12 h to replace the Na+ with H+. Secondly, the precursors of the H2Ti3O7 arrays were rinsed and dried at 80 °C in a vacuum oven, which were further calcined at 450 °C for 45 min at a ramping rate of 10 °C/min−1 under H2/Ar gas in a tubular furnace at a gas flow rate of 80 mL/min. This procedure resulted in the formation of nonstoichiometric TiO2 with abundant oxygen vacancies. Finally, TiO2 nanowire arrays with oxygen vacancies were formed (denoted as TiO2 NA-Vo). Similarly, TiO2 nanowire arrays (denoted as TiO2 NA) were acquired by calcining the H2Ti3O7 arrays in muffle furnace at 450 °C for 45 min at 10 °C min−1.

NCDs were prepared via a hydrothermal method [45]. Citric acid (3.0 g) and ethylenediamine (1.0 mL) were dispersed in 10 mL of deionized (DI) water, which was then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 5 h. The removal of large particles was achieved through the high speed (10,000 rpm/min.) centrifugation of the NCDs solution, which was then vacuum dried at 70 °C for 24 h. Finally, a 10 mg/mL NCDs solution was obtained through the redissolution of the NCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo and NCDs/TiO2 NA electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: NCDs were doped by immersing the TiO2 NA-Vo/Ti foam within different concentrations (0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8 mg/mL) of NCDs solutions for 24 h in the dark. Next, they were rinsed with ultrapure water, and dried with N2. Finally, the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo composites were annealed in a rapid annealing furnace for 20 min at 200 °C under N2 atmosphere. Similarly, the NCDs/TiO2 NA nanocomposites were obtained by immersing the TiO2 NA/Ti foam into NCDs solutions.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S4800), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100HR) were performed to investigate the morphologies and structures of the nanomaterials. The crystallinity of different samples was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a RigakuUltima III diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation over the 2θ range of from 10 to 90°. The chemical state and components of electrodes were investigated through X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS, Thermo VG Escalab 250Xi, INC). All binding energies were calibrated using contaminant carbon (C 1s = 284.8 eV) as a reference. UV–Vis-NIR diffuse reflectance (DRS) spectra were recorded with an UV2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu). The transient photoluminescence (PL) decay spectra was obtained using a HORIBA FluoroMax-4 fluorescence spectrophotometer and FLS980 fluorescence spectrometry (Edinburgh Instruments). A Thermofisher Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (Thermofisher, USA) was employed to record the FT-IR spectra in the range of from 4000 to 400 cm−1. The Raman spectra was acquired using a confocal Raman spectroscopy system (Renishaw Invia Raman microscope), which was equipped with a Leica microscope with 10, 50, 100× objectives, and 532 nm laser lines as the excitation source.

All measurements were carried out using a CHI 760e electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Shanghai, Inc.) in a standard three-electrode quartz cell. Various samples were grown on the Ti foam (2 cm × 4 cm) as the photoanode (working electrode), a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode, and a platinum plate electrode (with same area) as the counter electrode. A solution containing 0.1 mol·L−1 Na2SO4 (pH = 6.8) was employed as the supporting electrolyte. A 300 W xenon lamp equipped with a 420 nm cutoff filter was used as the light source, with the irradiation distance from the light source to the photoanode being set at 10 cm. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed at +0.6 V vs. SCE in the frequency range of from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz. The transient photocurrent response was collected by switching the light on and off every 30 s at an applied potential of 0.6 V vs. SCE under light irradiation (wavelength > 420 nm). All photoelectrochemical experiments were conducted at ambient temperature (25 °C).

The obtained potentials (vs. SCE) can be converted to RHE scale with the Nernst equation:
E
RHE
= E
SCE
+ 0.0591 × p H + 0.242 V
where ERHE represents the converted potential vs. RHE, ESCE is the obtained potential vs. SCE, and 0.242 V is the electrode potential of SCE at ambient temperature (25 °C).

For the incident photon to current conversion efficiency (IPCE) measurement, a 300 W xenon lamp (PLS-SXE300) was coupled with a monochromator, and the incident light intensity was measured with a calibrated Si photodiode. Here, the IPCE can be calculated from the photocurrents according to the following equation [46]:
IPCE =
J
ph
( m A ·
c m
- 2
)
× 1240
P
mono
m W ·
c m
- 2
× λ
( n m )
× 100 %
where Jph refers to the photocurrent density, λ is the incident light wavelength, and Pmono is the power density obtained at this specific wavelength.

The photoelectrocatalytic activities of different samples were evaluated through the degradation of tetracycline (TC) in an aqueous solution under light irradiation (wavelength > 420 nm). The typical photoelectrochemical degradation process was arranged as follows: vigorous stirring of a TC solution (70 mL) with an initial concentration of 50 mg L−1 using 0.1 M Na2SO4 as the electrolyte, was performed for 30 min in the dark to establish adsorption-desorption equilibrium between the photoelectrode and contaminant. The working electrode was irradiated with a 300 W Xe lamp. During the photoelectrocatalytic degradation process, 1 mL of the suspension was collected at predetermined time intervals and filtered through 0.22 μm Millipore filters. The changes in intensity of TC in the solution was analyzed by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). The HPLC was conducted using a Waters e2968 instrument equipped with a UV detector and a 4.6 × 250 mm Zorbax Eclipse XDB C18 reverse-phase column (Agilent, USA) at 25 °C. The detection wavelength of the UV detector was 355 nm and the injection volume was 20 μL. The isocratic mobile phase was comprised of 20% methanol and 80% water (with 0.2% formic acid) at a flow rate of 1.0 mL min−1.

During the photoelectrocatalytic degradation process, a series of free radicals, including h+,
OH, O2
− or 1O2 might be involved. To detect the active species generated in the photoelectrochemical system, various scavengers, including isopropanol (IPA, 10 mM), Na2C2O4 (10 mM), NaN3 (5 mM), and benzoquinone (BQ) (1 mM) were added to the solution. Photoelectrocatalysis under in situ visible light irradiation with +0.6 V vs. SCE was recorded using electron-spin resonance (ESR) spectra (JEOL, JES FA200, Japan) at a low ambient temperature (123 K). The main operating parameters were as follows: center field (324 mT), microwave frequency (9.055 GHz), microwave power (0.998 mW), and modulation frequency (100 kHz). The
OH, O2
−, and 1O2 were captured using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyridine-N-oxide (DMPO) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl (TEMPO) scavengers, respectively. A total organic carbon (TOC) analyzer (multi N/C 3000) was employed to analyze the degree of mineralization of the organic contaminant. Prior to injection into the TOC analyzer, the samples were sieved using a 0.45 μm Millipore filter. The photoelectrocatalytic degradation intermediates of TC were identified using a HPLC-MS system, which was equipped with an Agilent 1100 series HPLC coupled to a 6410 triple quadrupole mass spectrometer (Agilent Technologies, USA).

To satisfy the mentioned goal, TiO2 nanowire array with surface oxygen vacancies was designed and synthesized via a facile hydrothermal method, followed by hydrogenation in a reducing atmosphere (see Experimental Section for more details). Interestingly, a stable oxygen vacancy layer in TiO2 nanowire array was obtained following the NCDs introduction, which is illustrated in Scheme 1
. The crystallinity and phase composition of the as-synthesized samples were characterized by XRD as shown in Fig. 1 . The results revealed that two new characteristic peaks were observed at ∼2θ = 25.20°, 48.10° indexed to the (1 0 1) and (2 0 0) crystal planes of anatase (JCPDS No. 21-1272), which indicated anatase crystalline engineering owing to the arrayed structure grown on the titanium foam [6]. It was also demonstrated that the introduction of oxygen vacancies into TiO2 NA did not alter the crystal structure of the TiO2 NA. Further, the characteristic carbon diffraction peak was not detected in the NCDs/TiO2 NA sample, which was ascribed to the minimal content and low crystallinity of NCDs in the composites [45]. The precursor of hydrogen titanate grew densely on the titanium foam was shown in Fig. S1a, which akin to grass on a lawn. The morphology of precursor was the crucial step to form the nanowire array structure. The TEM image of the NCDs material is displayed in Fig. S1b, where spherical NCDs nanoparticles with average diameters of 2.8 nm were acquired (Fig. S1b inset). The PL spectra of the NCDs confirmed that they had the capacity to emit short light wavelengths (360–630 nm) subsequent to absorbing longer light wavelengths (400–900 nm), which revealed their superior fluorescent up-conversion properties (Fig. S2). The inset of Fig. S2 exhibited the down-conversion fluorescent properties of NCDs. For comparison, the left picture is the NCDs solution of certain concentration in the absence of light illumination, the right picture is that when the same NCDs solution was illuminated under 365 nm UV light, a bright blue luminescence was observed. These results indicated that the as-prepared NCDs were efficient energy transfer materials for enhanced solar light absorption, which was attributed to the capacity to transform low energy visible light and NIR light to shorter high energy wavelengths [47].

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was further carried out to observe the surface morphology and crystal structure of as-prepared NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo samples. Fig. 2 a and b depicts cross sectional and front face SEM images, which were obtained from the hydrogenation treatment of the precursor. In contrast to Fig. S1a, the array structure of samples were completely retained after rapid calcination. Meanwhile, the SEM images under high magnification revealed that the 1D array was composed of thin wedge-shaped sheets with small tops. The average widths of wedge-shaped nanosheets were measured to be 120–130 nm at the bottom, and 10–20 nm at the top, with a thickness of 50–60 nm at the bottom, 20–30 nm at the top, and a length of ∼1.2–1.5 μm (Fig. S3). The design of the wedge-shaped arrayed structure provided more reactive sites for small molecule pollutants which adsorbed to the electrode surface. The TEM image of the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo (Fig. 2c) confirmed the thin wedge-shaped structures of the 1D sheets, and a small number of NCDs can be observed. From Fig. 2d, the lattice spacing of NCDs nanoparticles was 0.21 nm, which could be assigned to graphite (1 0 0) lattice planes [48]. The HR-TEM image of NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo revealed that the NCDs nanoparticles were evenly dispersed on the surface of sample (Fig. 2e). The lattice spacing of hydrogenated TiO2 was 0.35 nm (Fig. 2f), which could be assigned to the anatase lattice planes [22]. Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) mapping of the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo further confirmed that the composition of elements, and clear Ti, O, C, and N signals were depicted in Fig. 2g–i.

The bonding energies of Ti and O atoms from NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo composites were investigated via XPS measurements. From Fig. 3 b, the Ti 2p spectra consisted of two individual peaks at 458.40 and 464.10 eV, which indexed to the Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 bonding energies of TiO2 NA, respectively. In contrast, Ti 2p peaks of NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo shifted to low binding energies of 457.95 and 463.65 eV, which signified the presence of Ti3+ [49,50]. Moreover, the spectra of O 1s was fitted into two peaks in TiO2 NA (Fig. 3c), whereas the peaks at 529.69 and 531.29 eV were ascribed to the lattice oxygen (TiOTi) and surface OH (TiOH), respectively [51]. Compared with the lattice oxygen peak of 529.3 eV in the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo, an obvious increase in intensity was observed, which might have been attributed to the generation of intrinsic deficiencies. A number of absorbed hydroxyl groups might dehydrate to form TiO bonds by combining with free Ti [52]. The results above indicated the presence of Vo, along with the appearance of Ti3+ and lattice oxygen deficiencies in the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo composites. Moreover, the full XPS spectra of NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo and TiO2 NA confirmed these elements existence (e.g., Ti, C, N, and O) in Fig. 3a, especially for the peak strength of C 1s and the signal peak of N 1s, which confirmed that the NCDs was introduced into TiO2 NA-Vo. High-resolution C 1s and N 1s are shown in Fig. S4, where the C 1s peak consisted of three distinct peaks with binding energies of 284.78, 286.47, and 288.98 eV, which were assigned to CC, CO, and CN, respectively [53]. To further verify non-stoichiometric oxygen vacancies within the TiO2 NA, the ESR analysis was performed at a low ambient temperature (123 K), which provided accurate data relating to trapped electrons and surface defects induced through the establishment of the oxygen vacancy layer. A single Lorentzian line centered at a g value of 1.999 in the ESR spectra was observed for the TiO2 NA-Vo and NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo (Fig. 3d). It should be pointed out that the ESR signal corresponding to oxygen vacancies in NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo was weaker than TiO2 NA-Vo. Combined with the state of Ti 2p in NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo, the peaks corresponding to Ti3+, initiated by oxygen vacancies, could be detected. This change indicated the potential modulation of structural defects subsequent to doping with NCDs, which stabilized the oxygen vacancy layer [22].

To more comprehensive affirm that the oxygen vacancies and NCDs had been incorporated into the TiO2 NA, the samples was characterized in detail by FTIR and Raman spectrum. From Fig. 4 a, the FTIR analysis revealed strong and wide peaks at ∼456–461 cm−1, 1636–1646 cm−1, and 3400–3500 cm−1, which were due to the existence of TiO, vibrations of surface adsorbed water with hydroxyl groups, and bending vibrations of OH groups, respectively [54]. A red-shift was produced following the introduction of Vo, which implied the deficiency of oxygen lattice and the formation of Ti3+ sites on the reduced TiO2 NA under a H2/Ar atmosphere [55]. Meanwhile, the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo sample exhibited new absorption bands at 1282 cm−1 and 1428 cm−1, which corresponded to the vibrations of CO and CH groups. This indicated that the TiO2 NA-Vo was successfully modified and functionalized by the NCDs during synthesis [26]. Raman spectra of four samples was depicted in Fig. 4b, where six active Raman phonons are shown in 3Eg (143, 195, and 638 cm−1), 2B1g (396 and 518 cm−1), and 1A1g (512 cm−1), which represented the typical anatase peaks of TiO2 based materials [55], in accordance with the results of XRD analysis. A slight red shift and broadening of the characteristic peaks were generated following hydrogenation, which could be ascribed to the intrinsic TiO2 defects induced by oxygen vacancies under a reducing atmosphere [55]. As seen in the inset of Fig. 4b, two typical characteristic peaks, located at approximately 1380 cm−1 and 1605 cm−1, corresponded to the D-band and G-band of carbon, respectively [47], which further confirmed the presence of NCDs within TiO2 NA-Vo.

Furthermore, the separation efficiency of photo-generated charge carriers following the introduction of NCDs into TiO2 NA-Vo was investigated via the steady-state PL and time-resolved PL decay spectra. Steady-state PL spectra of the four samples was excited under a 325 nm wavelength (Fig. 4c). Compared with TiO2 NA, TiO2 NA-Vo, and NCDs/TiO2 NA, the PL intensity of NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo decreased significantly. The reason could be explained that the formation of a stable oxygen vacancy layer induced by NCDs, which restrained the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes under photoexcitation. Further, time-resolved photoluminescence (PL) decays were investigated for TiO2 as a function of reaction time (Fig. 4d). After excitation with a laser every 30 s, the PL decay dynamics for samples appeared to follow a single exponential decay curve after the first 3 ns, which exhibited exponential decay with fast components (τ1, τ2) (Table S1). The longest lifetime represents the efficient electron-hole separation efficiency, which occurred in NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo. It is generally known that charge carriers with greater longevity enabled additional opportunities for the engagement of catalytic reactions, thus contributing to the enhancement of catalytic activity [50]. Above results further confirmed that the surface oxygen vacancies of TiO2 NA could be in situ stabilized by NCDs doped, which effectively improved the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs.

To estimate the capacities of the solar light absorption and conversion of semiconductor catalysts are of equal importance. UV–Vis-NIR spectra of the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo exhibited enhanced light absorption of the solar spectrum, from 200 to 2000 nm, with 88–93% absorption in the UV region, and broad absorption from the visible to NIR region (Fig. 5 a). The overall absorption of light across the solar spectrum was increased with a slight red shift, which was primarily initiated by the up-converted PL properties of the NCDs [18]. Meanwhile, the band gap of a semiconductor can be estimated from the Tauc equation [i.e., curve of converted (αhν)n/2 versus hν from the UV–Vis spectrum, in which α, h, and ν are the absorption coefficient, Planck constant, and light frequency, respectively, and a constant of n is determined by the optical transition properties of a semiconductor]. Therefore, the band gap (E g) of the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo was determined to be 2.83 eV, which was narrower in comparison to the value of 2.96 eV in NCDs/TiO2 NA, 3.02 eV in TiO2 NA-Vo, and 3.1 eV in TiO2 NA (Fig. 5a inset). To gain further insights into the changes of the energy band following the introduction of Vo and doping with NCDs, Mott-Schottky plots of different photoelectrodes were analyzed in Fig. 5b, where the flat-band potentials of the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo, NCDs/TiO2 NA, TiO2 NA-Vo, and TiO2 NA were estimated to be −0.58, −0.50, −0.52, and −0.40 V vs. NHE, respectively. Previous literature has demonstrated that the conduction band (CB) position of n-type semiconductors are roughly equivalent to their flat-band potential [56]. Thus, the conduction band (CB) position of the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo, NCDs/TiO2 NA, TiO2 NA-Vo, and TiO2 NA are calculated to be −0.58, −0.50, −0.52, and −0.40 eV, respectively. As estimated from EVB = Eg + ECB , the valence band (VB) edges of the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo, NCDs/TiO2 NA, TiO2 NA-Vo, and TiO2 NA were evaluated to be 2.25, 2.46, 2.50, and 2.70 eV, respectively, with the major band structures of samples shown in Fig. 5c. To support the above discussion, the valence band maximum (VBM) of the as-synthesized photoelectrodes was further estimated by linear extrapolation of the peaks to the baselines in the XPS-VB spectra, the results of the band edges essentially agrees well (Fig. S5). Thus it can be seen that the vital influence of the incorporation of NCDs not only enhance the TiO2 NA-Vo to solar light absorption and conversion capacities owe to its up-converted PL property, but also reduce the band gap of TiO2 NA-Vo by doping.

In principle, photoelectrocatalytic reaction is involved in the redox cycle process, charge transfer exhibits a positive relationship to performance. Electrochemical impedance spectra measurements (EIS) for the Ti foam-based electrodes with and without light irradiation further revealed the charge transfer properties between the photoelectrode and electrolyte. As illustrated in Fig. 6 a, following the incorporation of oxygen vacancies and NCDs, the electrical resistance of the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo was smaller than that of TiO2 NA-Vo, NCDs/TiO2 NA as well as TiO2 NA, as indicated by the smaller impedance arc radius [57]. Under light excitation, a much smaller charge transfer resistance on the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo photoelectrode surface resulted from the enhanced diffusion of electrons with high mobility. A chronoamperometric technique was subsequently employed to investigate the measure the photocurrent response of the materials grown on the Ti foam. Upon light excitation, the electrodes generated instantaneous photocurrents, which promptly returned to a steady state in the absence of light. As depicted in Fig. 6b, the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo electrode exhibited a photocurrent density of 0.56 mA cm−2, which was more than 1.5 times, twice, and 3 times than that of the TiO2 NA-Vo, NCDs/TiO2 NA and TiO2 NA electrodes, respectively. The results of transient responses to photocurrent revealed that the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo composites possessed a higher electron and hole separation efficiency than did the other four photoelectrodes, which was in alignment with the results of PL spectra. Meanwhile, the incident photon to current efficiency (IPCE), which could evaluate the photonic efficiency of photoelectrodes was shown in Fig. 6c. As a result, the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo photoelectrode exhibited extremely high IPCE at 1.24 V vs. RHE. These excellent properties of the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo photoelectrode, including EIS, photocurrent as well as IPCE, which indicated that the introduction of NCDs into TiO2 NA-Vo promoted the separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes, and accelerated the transfer of interfacial charges.

In our work, the photoelectrocatalytic activities of Ti foam-based electrodes were evaluated using tetracycline (TC), which has been reported to cause adverse effects on aquatic organisms [25], as a probe to assess their PEC degradation efficacies. Under light irradiation (wavelength > 420 nm) and an applied bias of + 0.6 V vs. SCE, TC with an initial concentration of 50 mg L−1 was degraded by 96% via the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo photoelectrode in 3 h (Fig. 7 a). This degradation followed pseudo first-order kinetics, with an apparent rate constant (k) of 1.1465 h−1, which was approximately two and three times faster than that of the TiO2 NA-Vo (0.5477 h−1) and the TiO2 NA (0.3010 h−1) photoelectrodes (Fig. 7b). Mineralization rate of TC during NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo electrode PEC degradation was estimated through the removal rate of total organic carbon (TOC), which revealed that ∼70% of the TOC could be removed in 3.5 h (Fig. 7c). More importantly, as a critical factor toward broader applications, the durability of the catalyst was evaluated. Remarkably, the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo photoelectrode exhibited superior catalytic stability, and essentially maintained the same activity level following eight cycles (Fig. 7d). In the meantime, regulating the concentrations of surface oxygen vacancies for TiO2 NA was achieved by altering the hydrogenation time. The PEC activities of the TiO2 NA with different concentrations of oxygen vacancies was shown in Figs. S6a and b, when the hydrogenation time was set at 45 min, the optimal concentration of oxygen vacancies in TiO2 NA was obtained. Other affecting factors, including the NCDs content and the applied bias voltage to the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo photoelectrodes also were investigated. As shown in Fig. S6c, when the TiO2 NA-Vo was loaded with NCDs of 0.4 mg/mL, the kinetic constant (k) of PEC degradation arrived at the maximum value. The influence of the applied bias voltage was very conspicuous, when the applied bias voltage was increased, the PEC activity of NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo photoelectrode initially increased, but then decreased. TC degradation rate attained a maximum value when the bias voltage was set at +0.6 V vs. SCE (Fig. S6d). A suitable external potential could effectively promote photogenerated electron transfer to the counter-electrode through an external circuit, which inhibited the recombination of photogenerated charges. When the bias potential was too high, the TC degradation rate decreased due to the reduced population of photogenerated carriers caused by the reallocation of the space charge layer [58]. In contrast, the degradation experiments with only PC and EC (applied voltage of +0.6 V vs. SCE) also were conducted. Just as expected, the degradation efficiency toward TC was considerably lower than the PEC.

Quenching experiments and ESR spectroscopy were subsequently performed to deeply understand the degradation mechanism and clarify the contributions of different reactive oxygen species (ROS) during the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo PEC degradation of TC. For this aspect, active species trapping experiments were conducted through the addition of various types of scavengers to the PEC system. For example, sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4) and isopropanol (IPA) served as hole (h+) and hydroxyl radical (
OH) scavengers, NaN3 as hydroxyl radical (
OH) and singlet oxygen (1O2) scavengers, and 4-benzoquinone (BQ) as superoxide radical (O2
−) scavengers. The results are shown in Fig. 8 a and Table S2, where the TC degradation rate was limited by the addition of IPA and NaN3, with contribution rates of
OH and 1O2 at 0.56 h−1 and 0.39 h−1, respectively. Particularly, the inhibitory effects of Na2C2O4 and BQ on the degradation of TC were apparent, and the rates were calculated to be 0.34 h−1 and 0.14 h−1, respectively. The direct PEC degradation rates with the addition of different scavengers are also shown in Fig. S7. Based on the results above, it could be concluded that h+,
OH, O2
−, and 1O2 played different degrees of roles in the PEC degradation of TC, respectively. To reveal the synergistic effect between oxygen vacancies and NCDs in the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo composites, in-situ ESR was employed to detect the changes of free radicals (
OH, O2
−, and 1O2) with the light irradiation time. Obviously, there were no ESR signals observed under dark conditions. Simulating the PEC environment under visible light irradiation with + 0.6 V vs. SCE, the strongest four-line ESR signals of DMPO-
OH and DMPO-O2
−were detected with intensity ratios of 1:2:2:1 and 1:1:1:1, respectively, which indicated that there were certain quantities of
OH and O2
− generated with increasing illumination time during the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo electrode PEC process. In contrast, during this period with TiO2 NA-Vo and TiO2 NA as photoanodes, the ESR signal intensities of DMPO-
OH and DMPO-O2
− decreased in turn (Fig. 8b and c). Signals from the TEMPO-1O2 with an intensity ratio of 1:1:1 were clearly obtained (Fig. 8d). Similarly, the signals increased remarkably following the introduction of oxygen vacancies and NCDs doping. These further confirmed the stabilized and regulated oxygen vacancy layer, induced by NCDs, which resulted in the consecutive generation of more free radicals, including
OH, O2
−, and 1O2.

In order to further reveal the vital roles that NCDs introduced into TiO2 NA-Vo for enhanced visible light-driven PEC activities, high-resolution XPS spectra of the Ti 2p and O 1s for NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo and TiO2 NA-Vo before and after 8 runs of PEC degradation of TC under visible-light irradiation were investigated in Fig. 9 a and b, respectively. Observing from the Ti 2p signals at 458.11 eV (Ti3+ 2p3/2) and the O 1s signals at 529.37 eV (TiOTi) of the used NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo hardly changed in contrast to pristine one, which indicated that the Ti3+ and the lattice oxygen (TiOTi) from NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo still remained after 8 runs of PEC degradation reaction [50]. Compared with TiO2 NA-Vo, the position of Ti 2p3/2 at 457.91 eV (represent for Ti3+) transformed to 458.31 eV (related to Ti4+), which implied that charge transfer from conversion of low-valence Ti3+ to Ti4+ after 8 runs of PEC degradation reaction [19], and the same trend also arose in the O 1s spectrum. It's remarkable that the oxygen vacancies signal of the TiO2 NA-Vo after 8 runs of PEC degradation reaction in the ESR spectra was weaker a lot than pristine one. However, the oxygen vacancies signal of the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo after 8 runs of PEC degradation reaction only receded a little (Fig. 9c). All of results affirmed that the introduction of NCDs in TiO2 NA-Vo could in-situ construct a stable the oxygen vacancy layer in TiO2 energy band, which made the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo materials hold more the defects state of Ti3+ as well as surface oxygen vacancies during the catalytic reaction. This resulted in a configuration favorable for the visible light-driven PEC degradation activities.

Thus, a potential mechanism for the PEC degradation of TC over the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo photoelectrode was illustrated in Scheme 2 . In this system, the PEC degradation process included the following three consecutive steps: light harvesting, charge separation/oriented migration, and surface adsorption and redox reaction. Under broad-spectrum irradiation, NIR light could be efficiently absorbed and converted to visible light through NCDs to achieve an augmented Vis-NIR photonic capturing capacity of the photoelectrode. With an external bias voltage, the electrons of the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo composites were transferred to Ti foam electrode, and arrived at the surface of cathode electrode (Pt slice) through an external circuit, which promoted charge separation and migration. In principle, matching the conduction and valence band levels of a catalyst with the redox potentials of reactants is the prerequisite for the generation of free radicals. For the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo catalyst, the holes (h+) on the valance band (VB) with 2.25 eV were more positive than the standard oxidation potentials of
OH/OH﹣(1.99 eV) [47], which was suitable for the oxidation of H2O/OH− to
OH. Photo-generated electrons on the conduction band (CB) with −0.58 eV were more negative than the standard redox potential O2/O2
− (−0.33 eV) [59], which could reduce O2 to O2
−. Further, the electrons on the NCDs were also able to combine with O2 to form O2
− [45]. Meanwhile, the singlet oxygens (1O2) that were formed through the O2
−reacted with h+ [60]. These generated reactive oxygen species (ROS) attacked TC molecules adsorbed to the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo photoelectrode interface, which left them completely decomposed and mineralized.

Subsequently, the degradation process of TC was analyzed in detail (Scheme 3 ). LC-MS techniques were employed for qualitative identify, with a comprehensive mass spectra analysis shown in Fig. S8. During the overall degradation process, four primary active oxidizing species, including hydroxyl radicals (
OH), holes (h+), superoxide anions (O2
−), and singlet oxygens (1O2) attacked different active sites of the TC molecules. According to the mass spectra, fourteen key products were identified as 460.15 (P1), 432.12 (P2), 430.14 (P3), 416.12 (P4), 416.16 (P5), 400.13 (P6), 426.14 (P7), 359.10 (P8), 278.16 (P9), 279.16 (P10), 242.06 (P11), 186.11 (P12), 122.04 (P13), and 102.04 (P14). Briefly, the TC molecule m/z = 444.16 was initially attacked by
OH to form its hydroxylated product P1 (m/z = 460.15). This intermediate was subsequently transformed to P2 (m/z = 432.12) via dealkylation [61]. Products with m/z of 430.14 (P3) and 416.12 (P4) resulted from the dealkylation reactions of TC, which were generated via O2
−/1O2 to attack the N-30 position of the TC, which caused the loss of the N-methyl group due to the low N–C bond energy [62]. Furthermore, the TC resident C-26 possessed a higher positive point charge, which implied that this position might be susceptible to O2
− attack. This could form the intermediate with a m/z of 416.16 (P5) via the loss of amino, and a further attack by h+ led to the generation of an intermediate with a m/z of 400.13 (P6) via dehydration [63]. Additionally, holes (h+) could attack the C-11 position of TC to generate an intermediate with a m/z of 426.14 (P7) via the loss of a hydroxyl group [61]. The ring opening products, including the compounds P11 (m/z = 242.06), P12 (m/z = 186.11), P13 (m/z = 122.04), and P14 (m/z = 102.04) were formed through the further oxidation of products P8 (m/z = 359.10), P9 (m/z = 278.16), and P10 (m/z = 279.16). Finally, all of these ring-opening products were oxidized to CO2 and H2O.

Lastly, environmental factors that the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo PEC degradation of TC faced during practical application were investigated, including pH value, water matrices, and natural water constituents. From Fig. 10 a and b, the fitting results showed that PEC degradation of TC followed first-order kinetics at different pH, and the rate constants at the solution pH value of 3, 5, 7 and 9 were measured as 0.4852, 0.8947, 1.1280 and 1.5665 h−1. With the increase of pH from 3 to 9, the increased rate constants indicated enhanced PEC activities of TC with increasing solution pH. This result can be explained that the TC is an amphoteric molecule with pKa values of 3.3, 7.7, 9.7 and 12 [64], as Fig. S9 showed, when TC molecules exist in neutral and positively charged forms at pH 4 and negatively charged TC molecules are dominant at solution pH 9, and photolysis of TC molecules could be markedly inhibited in TC+ form and promoted in TC− form [65,66]. PEC degradation of TC in different natural waters (DI water, WWTP effluent, pearl river water) were then carried out to assess the technical feasibility of the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo PEC process on the treatment of ambient TC wastewater. As showed in Fig. 10c and d, the degradation of TC was slightly inhibited in all the natural water matrices when compared to DI water. The rate constants of TC degradation were 1.1465, 0.9984, and 1.0221 h−1 in DI water, river water, and WWTP effluent, respectively. These inhibition effects might be due to the coefficient of co-existing HA, cations and anions that present in water matrices. For this, the natural water constituents (e.g. cations, anions, and dissolved organic matter (DOM)) were investigated in Fig. 10e. It is evident that NO3 −, Cl−, Ca2+, and Mg2+ exhibit inapparent influences for the PEC degradation of TC. On the contrary, transition metal ions (Cu2+) significantly restrain the degradation of TC. This suppressive effect on the degradation of TC might have been due to the electrons scavenging effects of transition metal ions, thereby reducing the formation of O2
− [45]. In addition, a representative of aquatic DOM, humic acid (HA) exhibited a enhanced the PEC degradation of TC compared with that the blank one. As a natural photosensitizer, HA consists of a large amount of chromophores and thus can form the excited state 3HA* upon irradiation [67]. 3HA* can further induce either the direct degradation of organic compounds via energy transferring, or the formation of the reactive species, such as
OH [68]. On the whole, the excellent removal performance of TC indicated that NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo PEC degradation technique could be used for the treatment of ambient TC wastewater.

In summary, we demonstrated that the incorporation of NCDs not only reduced the band gap of TiO2 by doping, but also constructed a stable oxygen vacancy layer in TiO2 energy band via in-situ stabilizing surface oxygen vacancies of TiO2 NA photoelectrode. As a result, the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo photoelectrode exhibited high photoactivity and durability, with a degradation reaction constant that was ∼two and three fold higher than that of the TiO2 NA-Vo and TiO2 NA photoelectrodes, respectively, and could remain high degradation efficiency after eight consecutive degradation cycles. Moreover, the NCDs/TiO2 NA-Vo electrode exhibited a photocurrent density of 0.56 mA cm−2, which was more than three times than that of the TiO2 NA electrodes. These excellent visible light-driven PEC activities could be ascribed to the stabilized and regulated oxygen vacancy layer induced by NCDs, which further accelerated the interfacial charge separation to generate more free radicals (e.g.
OH, O2
−, and 1O2), which was confirmed by ESR and PL. Briefly, our work provides an effective design for the regulation of the defected photocatalysts and photoelectrodes, while broadens the range of photoelectrochemical applications.
